item.content:encoded: I’m grateful that people like my dumb little comedy article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It forces the Foundation to intentionally make fools of themselves.

This has my love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP's like this one seem lazy to me. "Oh it's the Office haha +1" It's just a pop culture reference with no feeling to it besides the pretense that it's clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna say my opinion is the same here. I just don't see why the original owner would be a D-class. What would they have done to deserve living in a shitty cell? It's just not the Foundation's style to screw over random civilians imo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've got to agree with Legolizard here. It really sits wrong with me that the original owner is made into a D-Class (Something I heavily associate with canon fodder) rather than an extension of the SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this, I think it's simple and funny. When I rewatch the office for the 17th time I'll be remembering this whenever Jim decides to pull the look.

,':^|

Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agree to disagree based on your headcanon.

I don't subscribe to the Death Row Inmate Theory of D-Class because it's been pointed out many times that there aren't nearly enough criminals to keep the Foundation flush with guinea pigs. Those test subjects have to come from somewhere, and the original owner of the Object makes as good a Control for testing as any.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agree to disagree for two reasons:

A) -Js trend (not all of them) to be either metajokes about SCPs (Darkblade), something mundane as a joke like the USB you need to flip multiple times, or out there format stuff like Proscratinati or THE BALL.

2) Not all SCPs are horror; we have heartwarming and tragic SCPs, this just happens to be a comedy, but not at the expense of the format as -Js usually do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems more like a J-skip to me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, sooo the person was turned into a D-class because they where the original owner of 3302? That's not how D-class work and sets a pretty bad precedent that the Ethics council shouldn't ignore.

The whole point of having D-class be convicted criminals of life or death sentences is to ease the conscious of the staff at the foundation. If anyone can be turned into a D-class for simply coming into contact with an SCP than the moral and suicide issues that were prevalent in the early foundation could possibly return.

If anything, he should be refereed to as an instance of SCP-3302-1 or A.
The funny thing is that I’m more of a Parks and Rec guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: AAAaaaa now i see ha clever of you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, and in my mind, specifying where he’s looking each time he makes “The Jim Look” is signifying that there are actually “cameras” rather than him just looking aside at a random direction. The camera moves around in Office-style shows, so the placement changes as it would in a sitcom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
5 or 6 words to the interview to foreshadow it?
Where in interview was that?

fun simple scp +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m glad you liked most of it. :-)

My thought was that this is the original person who they found the anomaly with, so they are still in the early testing phases; I assume this is where they tested whether or not he realized that he was doing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I don't understand why Evans is triggering the anomaly every X seconds (or however often his watch beeps) during the interview. Is there something he's hoping to achieve or learn from doing this? It seems kind of out of place.

But that's about my only complaint. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was waiting for a faint short piano riff playing in the background at some point. Little sad that didn't happen but it still made me giggle so +1 for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funnily enough, everything written up except the final addendum was initially meant to be played straight; just a story of how shitty it would be to “Jim Look” in real life and have everybody know it and respond to it. (As far as I know, nobody responds to that in the show, but I’ve only seen ten episodes of the UK version and none of the US.)

That last addendum was a last minute gasp moment where my brain fired, “Oh my god, what if there really were cameras?” Admittedly, I did go back and specify exactly where D-3086 was looking to help set that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was with this right up until the final addenda, which felt like it was giving far too much away, ruining the clever subtlety in the interview. I don't really watch or know anything about The Office, but I was still able to glean what was going on prior to that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You picked an interesting time to post this. All throughout my read I kept waiting for murder to happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just love the idea of some multidimensional monstrosity writing excited fan letters and fangirling over a D-class.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
My first attempt at a short article with no gimmick. I hope you guys enjoy it!

My official audio adaptation can be found here:
https://youtu.be/FWy1vIs501Q

Some random thoughts about this SCP:

Coldpost challenge: I wanted to see if I could go start to finish without help and make it work.

Inspiration can come from anywhere: I had a eureka moment in the middle of a stress state after reading an r/SCP writing prompt about “The Office box set that forces you to binge the whole thing.” I obviously went a different route, but still…

Rule of three: Very basic version in the interview. Test, test, not test. Set-up, repetition, subversion.

Series IV Stereotypes: This is short. It’s not meta. It’s not a world ender or even that dangerous.

The Final Addendum/Twist: The bulk of the article was written to be a stand-alone story about how it would suck to have people know when you thought they were stupid because I don’t think they mention how rude that would be in “The Office.” The final twist came to me right at the finish and I think it adds a nice WTF Weird at the end, and I only had to add 5 or 6 words to the interview to foreshadow it.

Check out more from me over at TheeSherm’s Author Page and Site-42 Hub
